citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The licensee is not using appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries and has not
arranged for emergency veterinary care. Inspectors observed a brown and black Barbados Black Belly sheep in an
outdoor enclosure putting most of its weight on its front legs when walking and standing. A facility representative stated
the animal was born the day prior.
The animal was walking with severe lameness in both rear legs, worse on the right, with only intermittent weight bearing
on the right and obvious lameness in the left. When the animal had shifted its weight from walking back to standing, it
appeared unable to bear weight on the right for more than a few seconds, and therefore frequently stood only on the front
two limbs.
A facility representative stated that they had observed the animal's condition yesterday, but they have not notified the
veterinarian because they knew that the veterinarian was going to be out of the area, and they have not initiated any
treatments. The facility representative stated that they planned to monitor the animal. In the absence of regular attending
veterinary care, not ensuring emergency care is available to animals causes their medical needs to go untreated, causing
serious animal welfare and health concerns.
This animal must be evaluated by a veterinarian for treatment and the facility must use appropriate methods to prevent,
control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries, and ensure the availability of emergency, weekend, and holiday care.
Correct by 10:00 AM on Thursday, February 26, 2026.
This inspection was conducted with a facility representative and the exit interview was conducted with the licensee.
Additional Inspectors:
SCOTT WELCH, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2)  Direct
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
The licensee is not using appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries and has not 
arranged for emergency veterinary care. Inspectors observed a brown and black Barbados Black Belly sheep in an 
outdoor enclosure putting most of its weight on its front legs when walking and standing. A facility representative stated 
the animal was born the day prior. 
 
The animal was walking with severe lameness in both rear legs, worse on the right, with only intermittent weight bearing 
on the right and obvious lameness in the left. When the animal had shifted its weight from walking back to standing, it 
appeared unable to bear weight on the right for more than a few seconds, and therefore frequently stood only on the front 
two limbs. 
 
A facility representative stated that they had observed the animal's condition yesterday, but they have not notified the 
veterinarian because they knew that the veterinarian was going to be out of the area, and they have not initiated any 
treatments. The facility representative stated that they planned to monitor the animal. In the absence of regular attending 
veterinary care, not ensuring emergency care is available to animals causes their medical needs to go untreated, causing 
serious animal welfare and health concerns.  
 
This animal must be evaluated by a veterinarian for treatment and the facility must use appropriate methods to prevent, 
control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries, and ensure the availability of emergency, weekend, and holiday care.  
 
Correct by 10:00 AM on Thursday, February 26, 2026. 
 
This inspection was conducted with a facility representative and the exit interview was conducted with the licensee. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
SCOTT WELCH, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
